1: Daniel 8 begins the second Hebrew section (Daniel 8:1 – Daniel 12:13), resuming after the Aramaic block of 2:4b–Daniel 7:28. The verse marks the transition with an emphatic double self-reference: ʾēlay ʾănî dānîyēʾl ('to me, even to me Daniel') — the emphatic positioning of the prophet as recipient. The shift from Aramaic (the international court language) back to Hebrew (the covenant people's language) signals that this vision concerns Israel's eschatological history more specifically.
2: bĕšûšan habbîrāh (בְּשׁוּשַׁן הַבִּירָה): 'in Susa the citadel.' Bîrāh (Hebrew, from Akkadian bīrtu) = fortress, citadel, palace-complex. Susa was the Elamite capital and later the Persian royal winter residence (Esther 1:2; Nehemiah 1:1). Daniel is transported there in vision — physically in Babylon but visionary in Susa on the Ulai canal (ʾûbal ʾûlay), suggesting the vision concerns the Persian empire whose capital lies there.
3: ʾayil ʾeḥāḏ ʿōmeḏ lipnê hāʾûbāl wĕlô qarnayim: 'a ram standing before the canal, and it had two horns.' The Hebrew ʾayil (ram) is the specific term for adult male sheep — used in the sacrificial system (Genesis 22:13; Exodus 29:15-16) and in royal imagery. The two horns (qarnayim) are identified in Daniel 8:20 as the kings of Media and Persia. The higher-growing horn that grew up last mirrors Persia's eventual dominance over the Medo-Persian partnership.
4: pōreḥ yāmmāh wĕṣāpônāh wĕnegbāh: 'pushing westward and northward and southward.' Three of the four compass directions — the Persian empire pushed west (Lydia, Greece), north (Scythia), and south (Egypt, Nubia), but notably not east where the empire already extended. wĕkol-ḥayyôt lōʾ-yaʿamdû lĕpānāyw (no beast could stand before him) — the ḥayyôt vocabulary echoes chapter 7, connecting the vision with the four-beast sequence.
5: ṣāpîr hāʿizzîm bāʾ min-hammāʿărāb (צָפִיר הָעִזִּים בָּא מִן-הַמַּעֲרָב): 'a male goat came from the west.' Ṣāpîr = a he-goat (used in cultic contexts, Leviticus 4:23; Numbers 7:16). The goat approaches 'without touching the ground' (wĕʾên nōgēaʿ bāʾāreṣ) — capturing the extraordinary speed of Alexander's conquest (334-323 BCE), which subdued the Persian empire in under ten years. The qeren ḥāzût (conspicuous/notable horn) between his eyes is the first king of Greece (Daniel 8:21).
6: wayyiggaš ʾēlāyw bĕḥămat kōḥô: 'he came to him in his mighty fury.' Ḥēmāh (fury, wrath) + kōaḥ (strength, power). The goat charges the two-horned ram in a fury that matches the historical accounts of Alexander's ferocity in battle at Issus (333 BCE) and Gaugamela (331 BCE). The collision of ram and goat is one of the most vivid combat descriptions in OT prophetic literature.
7: wayyišbōr ʾet-šĕtê qarnāyw: 'he broke its two horns.' The shattering of both horns — the Median and Persian powers — in a single engagement. Wayyirpeśēhû (trampled it) — the defeated ram cast to the ground and trampled describes the utter humiliation of Persian power. Darius III fled repeatedly before Alexander; the Persian heartland fell without significant organized resistance after Gaugamela (331 BCE).
8: ûkĕʿaṣmô niśbĕrāh haqqeren haggĕdôlāh: 'when he was strong, the great horn was broken.' Alexander died at the height of his power (323 BCE) at age 32 — the 'breaking' (niśbĕrāh from שבר) of the great horn at its apex. The four replacement horns (ʾarbaʿ qarnôt ḥāzût) toward the four winds of heaven correspond to Alexander's four generals who divided the empire: Ptolemy (Egypt), Seleucus (Syria/Babylon), Cassander (Macedonia), Lysimachus (Thrace).
9: ûmin-hāʾaḥat mēhem yāṣāʾ qeren-ʾaḥat miṣṣĕʿîrāh: 'out of one of them came a little horn, which grew exceedingly great.' Miṣṣĕʿîrāh from ṣāʿîr = small/insignificant — the little horn begins small before becoming 'exceedingly great' (wayyigdal-yeter). It grows toward 'the beautiful/glorious land' (haṣṣĕbî = literally 'the gazelle/glory' — a poetic name for Eretz Israel, cf. Jeremiah 3:19; Ezekiel 20:6). Historically this describes Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175-164 BCE); typologically it points to a final eschatological figure.
10: wayyigdal ʿad-ṣĕbaʾ haššāmayim: 'it grew great, even to the host of heaven.' Ṣĕbāʾ haššāmayim (host of heaven) can mean: (1) stars/celestial bodies; (2) angelic beings; (3) Israel as God's covenant people. The trampling (wattirmāsēm) of the host parallels Revelation 12:4 (the dragon sweeping a third of the stars). The little horn's cosmic pretension — reaching to the host of heaven — makes explicit the divine-realm encroachment that the mocking of the Most High (Daniel 8:25) will verbalize.
11: hattāmîd (הַתָּמִיד): 'the regular/continual burnt offering.' Tāmîd is the technical cultic term for the twice-daily sacrifice (Exodus 29:38-42; Numbers 28:3-8). Antiochus IV abolished the tamid in 167 BCE (1 Macc Daniel 1:54), replacing it with swine offered on the altar. Śar-haṣṣābāʾ (Prince of the host) = YHWH as the Commander of the heavenly armies (Joshua 5:14) or the angel Michael (Daniel 12:1). The 'lifting up' against the Prince of the host is the blasphemous self-elevation of the little horn against God.
12: bĕpāšaʿ (בְּפָשַׁע): 'because of transgression/rebellion.' The giving over of the sanctuary to the little horn is understood as divine judgment on Israel's apostasy, not merely military defeat. The logic — Israel's sin created the opening for the desecration — connects with Daniel's prayer in Daniel 9, where he explicitly confesses the transgression that led to the exile and its continuation. Wĕhiślîk ʾĕmet ʾarṣāh (it threw truth to the ground) — truth as something that can be toppled.
13: hāppeša šōmēm (הַפֶּשַׁע שֹׁמֵם): 'the transgression that makes desolate.' The šōmēm form (causing desolation) is a participle from šāmam (to be desolate/appalled). This phrase is the prototype for Daniel's šiqqûṣ mĕšōmēm (abomination of desolation, Daniel 9:27; Daniel 11:31; Daniel 12:11), which Jesus cites in Mark 13:14. The question 'how long?' (ʿad māṯay) is the recurring cry of the Psalms (Psalms 13:1; Daniel 79:5) and of Revelation's martyrs (Revelation 6:10).
14: ʿereb bōqer ʾalpayim ûšĕlōš mēʾôt (עֶרֶב בֹּקֶר אַלְפַּיִם וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת): '2,300 evenings and mornings.' The phrase ʿereb bōqer (evening-morning) echoes Genesis 1's creation formula. Reading them as individual sacrifice-occasions = 1,150 days; reading as complete days = 2,300 days (≈ 6.3 years). Wĕniṣdaq qōdeš ('the sanctuary shall be restored/justified') — ṣādaq in Niphal = to be made right, vindicated. The Maccabean rededication in December 164 BCE (Hanukkah) is the historical fulfillment.
15: ûmĕbaqēš bînāh (וּמְבַקֵּשׁ בִינָה): 'seeking understanding.' Bînāh (understanding, discernment) is a key term throughout Daniel (Daniel 1:4, 17, 20; Daniel 2:21; Daniel 9:22; Daniel 10:1; Daniel 12:10). Before Daniel can understand the vision, he sees before him kĕmarʾēh-gāber (like the appearance of a man) — the human-form of the angelic figure that precedes Gabriel's appearance. The seeking of bînāh is itself a form of prayer that provokes divine response.
16: gabrîʾēl (גַּבְרִיאֵל): Gabriel — the first named angel in Scripture. His name means 'man of God' or 'God is my warrior' (from geber = strong man/warrior; ʾēl = God). He reappears in Daniel 9:21-22 (to explain the seventy weeks), Luke 1:19 (to announce John the Baptist), and Luke 1:26 (to announce the birth of Jesus). The commissioning voice from between the banks of the Ulai (ûbên ʾûlāy) is the voice of the commanding divine presence.
17: bĕʿēt qēṣ yihyeh hammārʾeh (בְּעֵת קֵץ יִהְיֶה הַמַּרְאֶה): 'for the vision is for the time of the end.' Qēṣ (end, appointed terminal point) is a technical eschatological term in Daniel (Daniel 8:17, 19; Daniel 11:35, 40; Daniel 12:4, 9). The angel's address — bîn ben-ʾādām (understand, son of man) — uses the full Hebrew form of the Danielic address, creating a deliberate echo of Daniel 7: the one addressed as 'son of man' is being given understanding about the Son of Man's ultimate victory.
18: wayyiggaʿ bî wayyaʿămîdēnî: 'he touched me and set me upright.' The angel's touch (nāgaʿ) restores Daniel from prostration to standing — the same gesture appears in 9:21 and Daniel 10:10, 18 as the standard angelic restorative touch. The sequence — prostration (overwhelming theophanic presence) → touch → standing — is the physical liturgy of prophetic reception, forming the background for Revelation 1:17 where Christ touches John with his right hand.
19: ʾaḥărît hazzaʿam (אַחֲרִית הַזַּעַם): 'the latter end of the indignation.' Zaʿam is the vocabulary of divine wrath/curse (Numbers 23:7-8; Psalms 78:49; Isaiah 10:5). 'The latter end of the indignation' = the eschatological period when divine judgment reaches its climax before the appointed end. The phrase implies that the horn's activity is itself a form of divinely-permitted retributive punishment whose duration is fixed at the qēṣ (appointed end).
20: ʾayil ʾăšer rāʾîtāh baʿal haqĕrānayim malkê māday ûpāras: 'The ram that you saw with two horns — these are the kings of Media and Persia.' Gabriel's interpretation is unusually explicit: the ram = Medo-Persia. This is one of the few places in OT prophecy where the symbolic figures are directly identified with named historical entities within the text itself, establishing the reliable historical-prophetic character of the vision.
21: ûṣāpîr hāʿizzîm melek yāwān (וְהַצָּפִיר הַשָּׂעִיר מֶלֶךְ יָוָן): 'the goat is the king of Greece.' Yāwān (Javan) = Greece — the name appears in Genesis 10:2 (son of Japheth) and throughout the OT for the Aegean Greeks. Wehaqqeren haggĕdôlāh ʾăšer bên ʿênāyw hûʾ hammelek hāriʾšôn: 'the great horn between its eyes is the first king' — Alexander the Great. The directness of the identification confirms the vision's historical anchoring.
22: wĕlōʾ bĕkōḥô (וְלֹא בְכֹחוֹ): 'but not by his power.' The four successor kingdoms inherit Alexander's empire but not his genius — wĕlōʾ bĕkōḥô (not by his power). The Diadochi kingdoms (Ptolemaic, Seleucid, Antigonid, Attalid) were perpetually at war with each other, never achieving Alexander's unified dominion. The fragmentation is itself part of the prophetic design, setting the stage for the little horn's emergence from one of the four.
23: melek ʿaz-pānîm ûmēbîn ḥîdôt (מֶלֶךְ עַז-פָּנִים וּמֵבִין חִידוֹת): 'a king of bold face, skilled in riddles/intrigue.' ʿAz pānîm (strong of face) = brazen, insolent, shameless — the opposite of 'lifting up the face' in respectful submission. Mēbîn ḥîdôt (one who understands dark/clever sayings) — ḥîdāh = riddle, enigmatic saying (Numbers 12:8; Judges 14:12; Proverbs 1:6). This king is both arrogant and intelligent in a deceptive way. Antiochus IV Epiphanes fits; the language is generic enough to describe the final antichrist figure as well.
24: wĕkōḥô yiʿăṣam wĕlōʾ bĕkōḥô: 'his power shall be great, but not by his own power.' The paradox: great power that is not his own. Where does the power derive? The grammar implies delegated or derived power — theologically, the permission of God for a period of judgment. Revelation 13:2 parallels this: 'the dragon gave him his power and his throne and great authority.' The king is a tool in a larger cosmic conflict.
25: bĕʾepes yād yiššābēr (בְּאֶפֶס יָד יִשָּׁבֵר): 'without hand he shall be broken.' Bĕʾepes yād (without hand/instrument) — divine agency without human instrument. The same 'without hand' language appears in Daniel 2:34, 45 for the stone cut out without hands that destroys the statue — confirming that only YHWH terminates this king's reign. Bĕlēb lĕbābô yagdîl (in his heart he makes himself great) — the double-heart idiom emphasizes deep self-delusion as the root of the blasphemy.
26: ʾĕmet hûʾ (אֱמֶת הוּא): 'it is true.' The vision's reliability is affirmed with ʾĕmet (truth/reliability) before its concealment. Ûsĕtōm ʾattāh hehāzôn (seal up the vision) — the instruction to seal (sāṯam) contrasts with Revelation 22:10: 'Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this book, for the time is near.' Daniel's vision concerns the distant future; John's revelation concerns the imminent present — the two commands define the difference in eschatological proximity.
27: waʾănî dānîyēʾl nihyêtî wĕnāḥaltî yāmîm: 'And I, Daniel, was overcome and lay sick for some days.' The verb nāḥāh (be sick, exhausted, faint) combined with nihyêtî (was done, overwhelmed) describes Daniel's physical collapse after the vision. The final clause — wāʾeštômen ʿal hammārʾeh wĕʾên mēbîn ('I was appalled by the vision and did not understand it') — is a remarkable honesty: even Daniel, after all of Gabriel's explanation, does not fully grasp what he has seen. Partial comprehension is the prophetic norm for eschatological revelation.
